It smelled of coal smoke and Sunday baking, so real she felt she could have stepped off the stair and walked down it. Doorways and windows firmed into being, warm and lit, a piece of Alderwick lifted briefly back into the world. But even as she marvelled, the root reminded her, gently, that this was only lent. The lane shimmered at its edges, not quite solid, held up by the small memory she had given and no more. The price was not yet truly paid. This was a taste, an offer, a demonstration of what the root could do if she gave it enough to keep for good.

For one long breath the root gave freely, and Rowan saw the full scale of what was possible. The single lane bloomed outward. Street joined street, and a whole quarter of the vanished town shimmered up into being around it, houses and shopfronts and a market square she had never known existed, all of it rising gently out of the spring at once. It hung there before her, trembling and luminous, an entire lost neighbourhood remembered back into the light for the length of a single held breath. Then it began to sink again, softly, as her small offering ran out. Rowan understood the promise in it. Everything the town had lost was still down there, whole, waiting, and the root would give it all back, if only she could pay a price large enough to match.

So it was a trade, plain and simple and terrible. Memory for memory. To buy back the town's lost years, Rowan would have to give the root memories of her own to keep in their place, the beloved traded for the lost. She crouched at the water with her greening seed and felt the shape of the bargain settle over her. She could measure the true cost of it now, press the root to show her exactly what such a trade would take from her before she agreed to anything. Or she could pull back here, snatch her hand from the water while she still had every memory intact, and keep herself whole at the town's expense. The lost quarter shimmered and sank in the spring below her, waiting on her nerve.

choices.label: Snatch your hand back and keep everything.

nodes.body: Rowan lost her nerve at the water's edge. The vision of that shimmering lost town had shown her how much the root could give, but it had also shown her how much it would take, memory for memory, and the size of the price frightened her. She snatched her hand back from the black spring and closed it into a fist. She would keep what was hers. Every memory of her own life stayed clenched tight and safe inside her, unspent and unshared. Rowan climbed back up the frozen stair into the mild afternoon with all of herself intact. But the town went on quietly losing itself around her, year by year, face by face, and she carried the knowledge of what she might have done like a stone in her chest for the rest of her days.

Rowan pressed the root to show her the whole cost, and it answered plainly. Whatever she gave, it would hold forever. This was the heart of it. The root did not borrow. Anything she surrendered to the spring she could never have again, not the taste of it, not the warmth, not even the knowledge that she had once owned it. A traded memory was gone as completely as the drowned faces below. She could follow this cost all the way to its end and choose with her eyes open, weighing each memory before she let it go. Or she could give something rashly, in a rush of feeling, and lose forever some piece of herself she had not meant to spend. The spring waited, cold and patient, for her to decide how carefully she would pay.

choices.label: Follow the cost to its end.
Let it take a memory you cannot spare.

nodes.body: In a rush of love and grief, Rowan gave too much. She let the spring take her whole memory of her grandmother, all of it at once, the Sunday kitchens and the birthday cards and the warm looping voice, poured down into the root in a single reckless offering. And the lane came back. A whole warm street of the old town rose solid and lasting into the world, bought and paid for. But Rowan stood in the middle of it a stranger. She could not recall why these houses should mean anything to her, or whose kitchen had smelled of apples, or why her own eyes were wet. She had traded the very person she was trying to honour, and now she wandered the restored lane emptyhanded inside, unable to remember what she had given to buy it.

Rowan understood the true price now, and it was heavier than any wall of stone. To restore the town, she would have to feed the root something she loved enough to genuinely lose. Not a spare memory, not a taste of Sunday she could shrug off, but something dear, something whose absence she would feel like a missing tooth for the rest of her life. That was the only coin the spring accepted. Small offerings bought small returns. If she wanted whole streets, whole years, whole lives lifted out of the dark, she would have to pay in things that mattered to her. Rowan knelt at the water with the greening seed in her hand and made herself look squarely at what the bargain would ask, no longer flinching from the size of it.

The arithmetic was plain, and it was cruel. The town could be bought back, memory for memory, life for life, exactly. And the buyer had to be someone the Quiet could not bill for its own, someone it could not simply reach in and drain. That was Rowan, and Rowan alone. Anyone born in Alderwick who tried to pay would only be swallowed themselves, adding to the debt instead of settling it. But Rowan stood outside the tree's reach. She could hand her own memories down as deliberate payment, and the root would take them as a fair trade and give the town back its stolen years in exchange. It was a terrible sum to be the only one able to pay, and she felt its full weight settle onto her alone.

The more she was willing to give, Rowan understood, the more of Alderwick would come rising back out of the deep. It was a straight exchange with no mercy and no discount. A small piece of herself would buy back a lane. A larger piece would buy a whole quarter. And if she gave enough, if she emptied herself sufficiently into the root, the entire lost town might climb back into the light, every vanished face and forgotten street restored. The limit was not the root's appetite. The limit was how much of her own life Rowan was prepared to spend. She rose from the spring with that knowledge sitting heavy in her, and turned to face the choice of how far she was willing to go, and who might stand with her when she did.

There was another way, and Rowan felt it call to her. The line of keepers had broken forty years ago, when her grandmother went down to the water and no one came after. That broken line had left one place standing empty, a single knot in the long rope of guardians tied and never filled. Rowan felt that empty place pull at her wrist like a thread drawn tight. The town needed a keeper. It had needed one for forty years, and the lack of one was why the Quiet had grown so bold, hunting freely with no one to tend and settle it. The vacancy waited, patient and open, and it seemed to know her name. She was the last keeper's blood, after all, and the empty place felt as though it had been left for her.

The empty place warmed against Rowan the way a living thing warms, eager, almost glad. She could feel how simple it would be. She had only to say yes, to step into the broken line and let the orchard take her as its keeper, and the whole terrible weight would lift from the town at once. Alderwick could forget its grief in peace, the way it had always wanted to, and the Quiet would settle and grow tame with a keeper to feed it again. One life, freely given, in place of the slow ruin of many. It pulled at her with a strange sweetness, this offer of a clean and total sacrifice. Rowan stood at the lip of the spring and felt how easy it would be to simply give herself away.

Rowan understood exactly what the empty place was asking of her. She could tie herself to it, step into the vacant knot in the line, and become the one the orchard remembered. She would be the keeper, the single willing name the root held so it need not devour the rest. The town would not have to be eaten face by face anymore, because it would have her instead, one life given wholly to the tree so that everyone else could keep theirs. It was the oldest bargain in Alderwick, the one the founders had made, offered to her now in the cold under the cider house. Rowan looked down into the turning water and weighed the enormous simplicity of it, one girl in exchange for a town.

But Rowan made herself look hard at what a keeper truly was. A keeper was remembered only by the root, and forgotten by everyone else. That was the whole shape of the sacrifice. She would hold the town together from below, unseen, while every living person above forgot she had ever existed, even Teo, even the faces she loved most. She would become another blank knot in the rope, another guardian no one could name. Rowan crouched at the water and felt the choice split before her. She could study that fate honestly, learn exactly what it would mean to vanish into the keeping. Or she could refuse it here and now, and decide flatly that no one, not even herself, should have to disappear that way.

choices.label: Decide that no one should have to vanish.

nodes.body: Rowan refused the keeper's place. No one should have to vanish like that, she decided, not her and not anyone, and she stepped back from the empty knot and would not fill it. It felt like the brave choice, the humane one. But refusing changed nothing about the root's hunger. Without a keeper to tend and feed it, the Quiet simply went on eating, as it had for forty years, only bolder now. Through the autumn and into the winter the forgetting spread and deepened. By the first hard snows even Odile, the last keeper on Mabb Hill, had thinned to a name that only Rowan still remembered. She had saved no one by refusing. She had only made certain there was no one left to hold the line at all.

Rowan pressed to understand the whole of it, and the truth was harder than a clean death. A keeper did not simply die. She was slowly erased from the living world, year by year, even as she held that world together from below. She would go on existing down in the dark of the root, remembered and preserved, while above her the people she loved forgot her face, her name, the sound of her laugh, until she was nothing to them at all. It was a long, patient unmaking, not a moment but a fading. Rowan could grasp the full weight of that fate and choose it with open eyes. Or she could tie herself to the knot right now, in fear and haste, before she truly understood what she was agreeing to become.

choices.label: Follow the cost to its end.
Tie yourself to the keeper knot right now.

nodes.body: Fear moved Rowan's hands before her mind was ready. She reached for the empty place and tied herself to it, stepping into the keeper's knot in a rush, desperate to end the town's suffering before she could lose her courage. And it worked, after a fashion. The town steadied at once, the Quiet settling as the root found a keeper to hold. But Rowan had bound herself in fear, without understanding, and the unmaking began immediately. Even as she stood there she felt herself thinning from the world above. Teo, climbing down to find her, looked straight at his cousin and could not quite place her face. She had become a keeper made of fright rather than choice, already fading from the eyes of everyone she had meant to save.

Rowan let the whole shape of the sacrifice come clear before her. A keeper was an anchor. By letting the orchard keep her, one willing person, the keeper held the whole town in place and spared it from being kept instead. It was a trade of one for many, the few dear years of a single life weighed against the countless years of everyone else. She could see the grim sense in it. A town could not go on losing itself forever, and a keeper stopped the bleeding by offering the root a single steady meal it could hold onto. Rowan knelt at the spring and understood, without flinching now, that this was the choice her grandmother had made, and every keeper before her, back through the long broken line to the founders at the water.

But a keeper was loved only by the orchard, and lost to everyone above. That was the part Rowan could not yet swallow. To become the keeper was to become a name that only a tree remembered, cherished by the root and blank to every human heart she had ever known. Teo would forget her. The town she saved would never know it had been saved, or by whom. She was not ready for that, she found, not like this, not chosen in the cold rush of a single desperate night. Perhaps one day she could give herself freely and whole, with a clear mind and an open heart. But not now, and not in fear. Rowan drew back from the empty knot, unready to disappear.

This was what the keeper's life truly was, laid bare at last. It was a whole existence spent as the thing that remembers, so that everyone else might be allowed to forget their grief in peace. The keeper carried the town's sorrow down into the dark and held it there, alone, forever, and asked for nothing back but to be the one who bore it. There was a terrible nobility in it, Rowan saw, and a terrible loneliness too. Her grandmother had walked into that fate. Someone always had, for two hundred years, so the market days could go on and the children could laugh unburdened. Rowan rose from the water carrying the full understanding of the price, and turned to face how, and with whom, it might finally be paid.

Now Rowan knew the price in full. The root would give the town back its stolen years, but only if it was handed something to keep forever in their place. That meant either a memory beloved enough to matter, or a keeper willing to be remembered by the orchard and forgotten by the world. Then she heard slow steps on the stair behind her. Word had reached the hill the way it always reached the keepers, and Odile Fenn, the last keeper on Mabb Hill, had come down at last, leaning heavily on Teo's arm. Her cousin had come too, refusing to let Rowan face the spring alone. And their being there changed the whole arithmetic. A price paid by one person was a wound, Rowan saw, but a price shared among many was only a scar. At the black spring, with the first frost gathering in the orchard above, the question was no longer whether she would pay, but how, and who would pay alongside her.

choices.label: Ask the whole town to share the weight.
Offer the root one thing to save one soul.
Bargain only to understand, not to win.

nodes.body: Rowan chose to share the weight as widely as she could. She sent Teo running back up through Alderwick to gather everyone who would come, calling out the names of the lost as he went, the names from the war memorial that no one could place. Bring them to the cider house before the frost, she told him, as many as will listen. She did not fully know yet how a crowd would help, only that the answer could not rest on her shoulders alone, and that Teo's stubborn kind of love, spread wide enough, might be the thing that held. She watched him scramble up the frozen stair and out into the dusk, and then she turned back to the waiting spring, listening for the sound of footsteps gathering above.

Teo came back breathless, and he did not come alone. Behind him crowded a dozen townsfolk, and then a dozen more, filing down the frozen stair with their breath smoking in the cold. They had not been given a reason they could understand. They had come, they said, out of a feeling none of them could quite name, a sense that tonight, of all nights, they must not forget on purpose. Old Mr Hallet was among them, and the frightened clerk, and faces Rowan had passed in the square. They gathered on the steps and the chamber floor, uneasy, shivering, drawn by the same pull that had always tugged at Rowan. The cellar that had been so lonely was filling with living warmth, and the spring seemed to feel it, its slow light quickening.

Rowan explained the plan as simply as she could. They would remember together, all of them, out loud and all at once. Each person would call up whatever they could still hold, and reach for what they could not, and they would do it as a single crowd rather than as scattered lonely hearts. That was the whole of it. If one person remembering was a candle the Quiet could pinch out, then a hundred remembering together might be a fire it could not smother. And crucially, no single heart would be drained to feed the root, because the weight would rest on all of them at once. Rowan looked around at the frightened, willing faces in the lamplight, and hoped it would be enough, and that she had gathered enough hands in time.

Rowan stood before the gathered town with the spring at her back, and two paths opened. She could try the harder, stranger thing: teach the root an entirely new bargain, a shared keeping in which the whole town held its own memory together and the tree kept through all of them instead of devouring one. That had never been done, and it might fail badly. Or she could take the gentler road, and simply tell these frightened people that the orchard had only ever been lonely and hungry, and let them make their peace with the smaller losses, keeping what they had and mourning no further. The crowd waited on her in the cold. Rowan felt the whole weight of the choice, the bold gamble against the quiet surrender, resting on what she said next.

choices.label: Let the town forgive the orchard and go home.

nodes.body: Rowan chose the gentler road. She told the gathered town the truth as kindly as she could, that the orchard had only ever been lonely, an old hungry thing that took their grief because they once begged it to. There was no villain here, she said, and no war to win. The townsfolk listened, and one by one they chose to live with what they had lost. They would keep the faces they still held and mourn nothing they could no longer name, making a quiet peace with a quiet grief. It was not a victory, and the vanished did not return. But the fear went out of Alderwick that night, and the town learned to live gently beside its old sorrow, neither healed nor hollowed, simply at peace with the shape of things.

Rowan chose the harder path, but she saw its danger at once. The weight of the town's whole memory had to land on enough hands at the same moment, or it would crush the few who were already there. If she began too soon, before the crowd had truly gathered, the burden would fall on a handful of shivering people and break them. Timing was everything now. She could hold to the plan and wait, agonising as it was, until enough of Alderwick had come down the stair to bear the load together. Or she could give in to the pressing frost and begin the ritual now, with too few hands, and pray it was enough. The cold deepened around them, and the spring's light pulsed faster, impatient.

choices.label: Begin before all the town has arrived.

nodes.body: The frost was closing in, and Rowan could not bear to wait. She began the remembering before half the town had arrived, with only a thin scatter of people on the stair. It was too few. The weight of all those lost years came up out of the spring and landed on the handful of hands that were there to catch it, and it was far too heavy for them. They staggered under it. Rowan watched in horror as the ones who had come reeled back, their faces going slack, forgetting even their own names under the crushing load. The chain she had tried to make snapped for want of enough links. She had moved too soon, and instead of sharing the weight she had simply broken the few brave souls who trusted her to time it right.

Rowan held to the plan and waited for the crowd to swell, and Odile taught her what to do. From the stair the old keeper explained, in her steady blind voice, how to teach the root a keeping spread across a hundred hearts instead of devouring a single one. It was an old knowledge, she said, that the keepers had guarded and never dared to use, because it needed a whole town willing to remember on purpose, and Alderwick had never been willing before. Now, at last, the people had come down of their own accord. Odile guided Rowan through the shape of it, the words and the intention, while the crowd gathered thicker on the steps and the spring waited, and Teo moved among the frightened townsfolk, keeping them calm.

Odile's voice reached them all from the top of the stair, clear and unhurried, teaching the old words. They were words that turned a devouring into a keeping, that changed a hunger fed by one poor soul into a memory held gently by all. Rowan repeated them, and the crowd took them up after her, uncertain at first and then steadier, a low chant rising in the cold chamber. The spring stirred at the sound of it. This was not the lonely bargain of the founders, one name given in the dark. It was something new, a whole town leaning down to the water together and offering to carry its own remembering, so that no single person need ever again be swallowed to keep the rest.

The plan was clear now, and it was beautiful in its way. The living would hold the town's memory between them like links in a long chain, each person carrying a small share, none bearing more than a heart could stand. And the root, instead of hollowing out one victim after another, would keep its long memory through the many, drawing a little from everyone and draining no one. It was the difference between a tax and a sacrifice. Rowan looked around the crowded chamber at all the willing, frightened faces, at Odile on the stair and Teo at her side, and felt the shape of a mercy the founders had never imagined. She turned to the spring, ready to seal the new bargain, the whole gathered town breathing at her back.

Rowan knew she could not empty the whole spring by herself. The lost town was vast, and she was one girl with one heart to spend. So she chose instead to save one. She would not try to lift a hundred faces out of the dark and fail. She would lift a single soul, completely, and let that be enough. Rowan knelt at the lip of the black water and fixed one face firmly in her mind, holding it there against the slow pull of the current below. If she could give the root a fair price for just this one, then one whole life might climb back into the light of Alderwick, and one family might be made less empty. It was small. But it was possible, and it was real.

The face Rowan fixed on was the neighbour girl. She had come back to her slowly, in fragments, as she stood by the water: a gap-toothed grin, a loud laugh, the way she had always won at conkers in the school yard and crowed about it for days. A whole small life surfaced in Rowan's memory, a girl the town had lost the very same winter that Rowan herself was taken away to grow up elsewhere. Perhaps that was why Rowan still had her when no one else did. They had been children together for a few short years, and then Rowan had left and the girl had drowned into the Quiet, and no one in Alderwick had mourned her because no one could remember she had ever laughed.

It was that girl Rowan meant to lift back into the light, no matter what it cost her. She held the gap-toothed grin steady in her mind and would not let the current take it. All the vast grief of the drowned town pressed around her, a hundred pleading faces, but she narrowed everything down to this one. One girl. One rescue she could actually manage. Rowan pressed her greening seed to the black water and reached toward the single life she had chosen, shutting out the rest so that her whole strength could go to lifting one soul clear of the dark. She would not save everyone. She had made her peace with that. But she would save this one, and do it right.

The root stirred, willing to deal, and Rowan felt the choice sharpen. She could brace herself to trade away a real piece of her own heart, a memory she loved, in exchange for lifting the one girl back into the world. That was the true bargain, and it would cost her something she could not get back. Or she could hold back from paying at all, and ask the spring only to show her the girl's face, to learn who it was she would have saved, and then carry that knowing back up into the daylight without spending anything. Rowan knelt with her hand on the cold water, the gap-toothed grin bright in her mind, weighing the safe knowing against the costly rescue.

choices.label: Only ask the root to show you her face.

nodes.body: Rowan found she could not quite bring herself to pay. Instead she asked the spring only to show her, clearly, the one she would have saved. The water answered. It lifted the neighbour girl's face to the surface, whole and laughing and gap-toothed, and held it there for Rowan to see. She looked for a long time, learning the face by heart, the name, the loud laugh, the small fierce life the town had lost. Then the girl sank gently back into the dark. Rowan climbed the stair carrying the knowing and nothing else, having taken nothing from the water and given nothing to it. She was changed by what she had seen, and she would carry that face for the rest of her life, but the girl herself stayed lost.

The root named its price, and it was fair and exact. One memory of Rowan's own, freely given, in exchange for the one soul lifted back. A life for a loved thing, clean and even. Rowan understood she could pay it honestly, handing over something dear so the girl could walk Alderwick again. That was the bargain the root offered, and it would keep its word. Or she could try to cheat it, to demand the girl back while surrendering nothing of her own, gambling that the root would give freely if she only pushed hard enough. Rowan felt the temptation of it, the wish to save the girl and keep herself whole. But the spring was old, and patient, and it did not bargain the way the desperate hoped.

choices.label: Demand the root give without taking anything.

nodes.body: Rowan tried to cheat the root. She reached into the spring and pulled at the neighbour girl with all her strength, meaning to drag her free while surrendering nothing of herself, giving the water no payment at all. The root closed hard against her. It would not be robbed. It held onto the girl and would not yield an inch, and it kept, as well, the long hour Rowan wasted straining at the surface with nothing to offer. When at last she gave up and drew back, exhausted and empty-handed, the girl was gone deeper than before, and the frost was closing over the water. Rowan surfaced having saved no one. She had wanted the rescue without the cost, and the old root had simply waited her out and kept them both.

Rowan braced herself to pay the fair price. She would give up one precious thing of her own so that one lost person could walk Alderwick again, remembered by everyone as if she had never gone. It was a clean trade, and she chose to make it with her eyes open. She searched her own memory for something dear enough to be worth a life, and found it, and held it ready to hand down into the water. Her heart ached at the thought of losing it. But the neighbour girl's laugh was worth an ache, Rowan decided, and more. She knelt at the spring with the greening seed in one hand and her offered memory in the other, ready to trade the beloved for the lost.

One rescue, complete and clean, was a candle lit against a darkness she could never hope to flood with light. Rowan understood that now, and she made her peace with it. She could not empty the whole spring, could not lift every drowned soul back into the day. But she could save this one girl wholly and well, and that was not nothing. A single life fully restored was worth more than a hundred half-attempts that saved no one. She would not let the size of the darkness talk her out of the one small light she could actually kindle. Rowan steadied herself at the water, the gap-toothed grin bright in her mind, and decided that a single saved life was not too small a thing to matter after all.

It was a small and complete mercy, offered into a sea Rowan knew she could not empty alone. She held that truth without bitterness. The lost town would stay mostly lost, and the root would go on drinking, and one day someone else might have to face this water. But tonight, this hour, she could lift one girl clear of the dark and give her back to the light and to the town that had forgotten her. One family made whole. One laugh returned to Alderwick. Rowan gathered herself at the lip of the spring, the greening seed warm in her fist and the beloved memory ready to give, and turned toward the water to make her small and shining trade before the frost could close the way.

Rowan decided she did not need to defeat the Quiet at all. She had been thinking like a soldier, looking for a way to win, and perhaps that was the wrong shape for this entirely. The root was old and grieving, not evil. What she needed was not victory but understanding, enough of it to live honestly beside the thing rather than forever fighting it. She let go of the wish to conquer. Instead she knelt at the water with an open mind and asked it, quietly, to help her understand. If she could truly grasp what the orchard was and why it hungered, then maybe she and the town could learn to share Alderwick with it, eyes open, no longer lying to themselves about the price of their easy harvests.

The root answered by showing her the founders again, and this time Rowan saw them differently. They were not villains who had sold the town for greed. They were parents, ordinary and desperate, who had buried too many children in too many hard winters and could not bury one more and still get up in the spring to prune the trees. They had been drowning in grief. So they had made a terrible mercy of forgetting, begging the orchard to carry what they could not. Rowan watched their grey faces at the water and felt her anger soften into something more like pity. They had not meant to doom their descendants. They had only been trying, in the one way they could find, to survive their own unbearable sorrow.

So Rowan asked the root not for years, and not for names, but for the whole truth. She wanted to understand the bargain the founders had made in their grief, all of it, the need and the cost and the long slow ruin that followed. Not to undo it, and not to win against it, but simply to know. She cupped the black water in her greening hand and opened herself to whatever it would tell her. Show me, she said silently to the old tree. Show me what really happened here, and what it has meant, and what it has cost you to carry it alone all this time. The spring grew still around her fingers, considering, and then, slowly, it began to open its long memory to her.

The root offered Rowan a choice in the way it answered. She could accept an even trade, a single year of the town's given back for a single year of her own, an honest small exchange between herself and the tree that took nothing by force. Or she could reach for something larger and more dangerous, and try to force the whole hard truth of the bargain into the minds of the entire town at once, making everyone understand what she now understood. Rowan knelt at the water and weighed them. The even trade was modest and safe and honest. The other was a reckoning, and reckonings, she was learning, could break the people who were not ready for them.

choices.label: Take back one year on the roots own terms.

nodes.body: Rowan accepted the old bargain on its own honest terms. She asked the root for a single year back, and she chose the year she was born here, in Alderwick, before her family took her away. The root considered, and agreed. It gave her that year, the town as it had been when she came into it, warm and whole and hers. And in fair exchange it kept a year of her own, quietly, without cruelty. It was an even swap, bittersweet and clean, and Rowan rose from the water feeling at once fuller and lighter, richer by one recovered year and poorer by one she had given. She had not beaten the Quiet. She had simply dealt with it honestly, once, and understood it better for the trade.

Rowan saw the danger in the larger path clearly. The root's long memory was a grief no single mind could hold alone, two hundred years of buried children and hard winters and quiet thefts, all tangled together. She could carry that knowledge wisely, letting it change her and guide her without crushing anyone. Or she could pour the whole of it, unasked, into the minds of the town, forcing on them a grief they had spent generations learning not to feel. The first was patient and kind. The second was a flood, and floods drowned people. Rowan knelt with the terrible weight of the root's memory pressing at the edges of her mind, and understood that how she carried it mattered as much as whether she carried it at all.

choices.label: Force the whole truth into every mind at once.

nodes.body: Rowan could not hold so much grief alone, and in her need she pushed it outward. She tried to pour the root's whole long memory into every mind in Alderwick at once, all two hundred years of buried sorrow handed to the town entire. It was too much. The people reeled under it, clutching their heads, unable to bear a grief given to them whole and without warning. They had spent generations learning not to feel exactly this, and now it fell on them like a collapsing roof. One by one they turned from her, shutting their minds, choosing the old comfortable forgetting over a truth too heavy to carry. Rowan had meant to make them understand. Instead she had shown them why the founders chose to forget in the first place.

Rowan chose to carry the truth wisely, and the spring opened its long memory to her alone. It came slowly, so as not to break her: the hard winters one after another, the small graves, the founders kneeling at the water, the long centuries of keepers walking willingly into the dark. She saw the mercy and the theft of it all tangled together into one old wound that had never once been allowed to heal. It was not a monster's memory. It was a family's, vast and sorrowful and human. Rowan held it as steadily as she could, letting it fill her without flooding her, understanding at last that the thing beneath the orchard was made entirely of the grief the town had refused, for two hundred years, to feel.

Rowan meant to meet the oldest tree the way you meet an old grief in a family, the kind that has sat unspoken at the table for generations. Not to defeat it, and not to pretend it away, but to finally say aloud the names it had carried alone for two hundred years. Someone had to speak them. Someone had to sit down with the sorrow and acknowledge it, name the buried children and the lost keepers and the vanished neighbours, so that the carrying could at last be shared. That was what the root had always wanted, Rowan understood now, underneath the hunger. It wanted its long grief to be witnessed. It wanted, after all this time, simply not to be alone with what it held.

Carrying that understanding, Rowan turned to face the oldest tree not as an enemy to be beaten but as the keeper of a sorrow the whole town would finally have to own. She would not fight it, and she would not flee it. She would stand at its root and acknowledge what it had carried, and invite Alderwick, gently and in its own time, to begin carrying a share. The grief was theirs, after all. It always had been. The tree had only been holding it in trust, alone in the dark, waiting two hundred years for someone to come down and help it bear the weight. Rowan knelt at the spring with that purpose steady in her, and made ready to speak the truth aloud to the waiting water.

Every path had brought Rowan to the same place at last. She knelt at the black spring beneath the oldest tree, and far above, through the cellar and the orchard, the first frost was silvering the grass. Behind her stood the gathered town, or else a single beloved face was held steady in her mind. The withered seed in her palm was green and whole now, a living shoot, the keeper's token woken and ready. And the root waited, ancient and patient, prepared to make its bargain. This was the hinge of everything. Here the Quiet could be turned from a hunger into a keeping, from a theft into a shared remembering, or else lost again for another forty years. Whatever Rowan had chosen to carry down to the water, the town's whole memory, a single name, an honest grief, she had to offer it now, and see what the orchard would choose to give back.

choices.label: Anchor the whole towns memory at once.
Bind the shared vow of the living into the root.
Save the one soul you came to lift.
Accept the bittersweet trade and let go.

nodes.body: Rowan pressed the greening seed to the black water and spoke the founders' bargain backward. Not the orchard keeps for us, she said aloud, her voice steady in the cold, but we keep for the orchard. The words rang off the wet stone. The root shuddered at them, the whole taproot trembling in the spring as though she had touched a nerve. And then it answered. A hundred living memories came pouring down the cellar stair at once, rushing from the gathered town toward the water, a torrent of held remembering flooding into the chamber. It was more than Rowan had bargained for, a great river of the town's love and grief and history breaking loose all together. She could feel it dragging at her, enormous and wild. She could steer this flood if she had help, or be swept under by a current far too strong for one girl alone.

choices.label: Steer the flood of memory together.
Falter as the current grows too strong.

nodes.body: The flood of remembering was more than Rowan could steer by herself. She reached for it, tried to gather it and aim it into the root, but it was a river and she was one pair of hands. It broke past her and scattered wide before it could anchor into anything lasting. The town above went dazed and reeling, half-remembering, half-lost, caught between two states. And the root drank the loose edges of the flood and was fed just enough to grow quiet and sleepy again, its hunger dulled but not answered. The great chance had been spent. Alderwick was left in a fragile, partial calm, neither restored nor wholly robbed, and Rowan understood too late that a current that size had needed more than her hands alone to guide it safely home.

But Rowan was not alone. Odile's worn hands closed over hers on the seed, and Teo's voice rose behind them, leading the frightened crowd, steadying the flood into something they could all carry together. With the three of them and the whole town pulling as one, Rowan turned the torrent toward the root and taught it the new bargain. And the spring changed. It had been turning like a drain, pulling everything down and away into the dark. Now, slowly, it began to turn the other way, like a wheel, like a millwheel catching the stream. The root stopped swallowing the town's memory and began instead to hold it, to keep it safe on the town's behalf rather than devouring it. The hunger was becoming a guardianship. Rowan felt the whole terrible engine of the Quiet reverse itself under her hands, and she steadied it, and held.

choices.label: Seal the new keeping into the root.

nodes.body: Rowan asked the gathered town for something stranger than sacrifice. She did not ask them to give up anything they loved. She asked them only to promise, together and out loud, to remember. Say it with me, she told them. We will keep them. We will not let them go again. The crowd took up the words, uncertain at first and then together, and the vow rose off them like warmth coming off a fire, a real and rising heat in the cold chamber. And the root leaned toward it. Rowan felt the great taproot shift in the spring, drawn not by hunger this time but by curiosity, tilting toward a thing it had never been offered in two hundred years: a gift, freely given, that it had not had to steal. The old tree seemed almost to wonder at it.

choices.label: Bind the vow into the root.
Lose the crowds nerve at the last moment.

nodes.body: The spring stirred, and the crowd's nerve failed. As the black water woke and the root leaned toward them, the gathered townsfolk grew afraid. This was too strange, too much, and one by one they lost their courage and began to climb back up the stair toward the safe ordinary light. The vow faltered on their lips and went unfinished. Rowan was left kneeling at the water alone, holding a promise that no one would stay to complete, the words dying in the cold. Without the whole town to carry it, the shared keeping could not take hold. The root, offered a gift and then abandoned before it was given, settled slowly back into its old and patient theft. The chance had come, and the town had climbed away from it, and Alderwick went on quietly forgetting itself.

But this time the town held. The shared vow spread thin across every heart in the crowded cellar, each person carrying only a small light share of it, so that no single one of them was drained or hollowed. That was the whole secret of it. A promise borne by one would have crushed that person. A promise borne by a hundred rested on each of them as lightly as a breath. And the root, offered at last a keeping it could hold without emptying anyone, accepted the new bargain the way dry ground accepts the first rain. It drank the vow gently, gratefully, and asked for nothing more. Rowan felt the change move through the water like warmth through cold hands. The tree would keep the town now, and the town would keep the tree, and no one at all would have to vanish for it.

choices.label: Seal the shared keeping for good.

nodes.body: The root accepted the shared keeping, and beneath Rowan's hands the split founding wall began to knit shut, living bark creeping over the old crack until the stone was whole and sealed. And up in the orchard, and down through all the streets of Alderwick, the vanished years came rising back like dawn filling a valley. Streets she had never seen unrolled into being. Faces returned to the memorial and to the kitchen chairs. The bakery stood warm and lit on the square once more, and among the returning crowd a gap-toothed girl who had once won every game of conkers laughed as though she had never been away. All of it came back at once, held now by everyone who had chosen to remember. Rowan rose from the sealed wall, and turned to see what the great restoration had, and had not, reached.

choices.label: Watch Alderwick wake into its whole self.
Notice the one gap the flood did not reach.

nodes.body: Alderwick woke whole. Every stolen year had come rising back, and now it was held safe by a town that remembered on purpose, together, refusing ever again to let its own people slip quietly into the dark. The orchard still stood on the hill, and it still bore its heavy fruit, but it fed on the town's freely given memory now and no longer ate its keepers. Rowan walked up out of the cider house into a square full of morning light and full of names that finally fit their faces. Neighbours greeted neighbours they had forgotten for forty years. The war memorial meant something again. She stood in the middle of it, tired to the bone and quietly amazed, the girl from away who had taught a whole town how to keep itself, and how to stop trading its heart for an easy harvest.

One face had not come back. As the restored town rejoiced above, Rowan searched the returning crowd and could not find her grandmother anywhere. Of all the drowned, hers was the memory the root had held the longest and the deepest, sunk down through forty years to the very bottom of the dark, and the great rising flood of remembering had passed her by. She was still down there, the last keeper, alone in the quiet. Rowan stood at the healing wall and understood that she had one choice left to make. She could leave her grandmother in peace, resting where she had chosen to go, and keep the rest of the town safely whole. Or she could reach once more into the deep, for the one soul the flood had not lifted, and try to bring her home too.

choices.label: Let her rest and keep the rest whole.

nodes.body: Rowan reached one last time into the deep. She sank her greening hand into the healing spring, down past the settling water, and found her grandmother waiting at the very bottom of the dark. She took hold, and she lifted, and the old keeper came up out of the black water at last. Her grandmother opened her eyes in an orchard that suddenly, all at once, remembered loving her, forty years of forgotten devotion flooding warm through the whole town. The two of them stood together in the restored orchard under the silvering frost, the long debt finally paid in full, the broken line mended, the family whole again. Rowan held her grandmother's hand and did not let go, and above them the oldest tree stood quiet and content, keeping nothing now that anyone still needed back.

Rowan let her grandmother rest. She had gone down to the water of her own free will, long ago, to save a town that could not save itself, and Rowan understood at last that to drag her back might be to unmake the very gift she had given. So she left her where she had chosen to be. The root remembered her now with honour, as the keeper who had bought the town its second chance, and Alderwick lived on whole and bright around that single gentle absence. It was a scar the town would carry with love instead of forgetting. Rowan climbed up into the restored square, and though her grandmother was not among the faces there, she was held safe in the orchard's long memory, and in Rowan's, honoured rather than lost, kept rather than eaten.

Rowan held one face steady in her mind, the neighbour girl she had grown up beside, lost to the Quiet the same winter Rowan's family took her away. She could still see the gap-toothed grin, still hear the crow of triumph over a won game of conkers. That was the one she would lift. She could not empty the whole spring, but she could pay honestly for a single life. So Rowan chose a memory of her own to offer in fair trade, one bright thing she could bear to lose, and held it ready over the water. One soul, one price, clean and even. She knelt at the black spring with the green seed in one hand and the offered memory in the other, ready to buy back the girl the town had forgotten it ever loved.

choices.label: Give the memory freely for the one.
Hesitate over which memory to lose.

nodes.body: Rowan hesitated a moment too long. She could not decide which piece of herself to give, weighing one memory against another while the cold deepened around her, and the moment slipped away. The frost came first. It closed over the surface of the spring like a lid of grey glass, sealing the water and the bargain shut for another forty years. The neighbour girl stayed down in the dark, a face in a fading photograph that no one but Rowan would mourn. She had been so close, one honest choice away from saved, and the nearness of it was the hardest part to carry. Rowan climbed the stair into the frozen morning with her memories all still intact and the one life she might have bought lost for another long turn of years.

Rowan chose what to give, and gave it freely. She let the root take her own first summer in Alderwick, a small bright handful of days from before her family moved away, a thing she could spare more easily than most. The root took it gently, without greed, lifting it out of her and leaving only a faint cool space where it had been. And then it gave her the girl. The neighbour girl rose up out of the black water, blinking and alive and whole, gasping as though surfacing from a long dive. She was remembered again, real again, restored to a town that had mourned her for years without ever knowing why its winters felt so empty. Rowan steadied her by the arm. The trade was done and fair, one summer of hers for one whole life returned, and she did not regret it for a moment.

choices.label: Bring the one you saved up into the light.
Ask if you might save one more.

nodes.body: Rowan climbed the cellar stair with the neighbour girl beside her, up into the frost-bright morning and the waking square. And the town gasped to remember her. Neighbours who had passed her fading photograph a hundred times without a flicker suddenly knew her name, her laugh, the games she had won, and they crowded around her weeping with a joy they could not explain. The Quiet still slept below in its spring, and the wider loss of the town remained, all those other faces still turning in the dark. But one whole life walked Alderwick again in the sunlight, laughing, real. Rowan had bought her back with a single summer she would never miss half as much as this one bright life was worth, and she watched the girl run into the crowd and knew it was enough.

The root would give no more than the fair exchange, one life for one memory, and Rowan chose to accept it rather than bleed herself dry. She could have gone on trading piece after piece of herself, chasing soul after soul, emptying her own life in a doomed attempt to save them all. Instead she stopped. She had saved one, wholly and well, and she let that be enough. Rowan walked home through the frosted lanes with the neighbour girl at her side and a hard, gentle wisdom settling into her. Saving one person completely, she understood, was worth more than failing at saving everyone. The town was not whole, and might never be. But one girl who had been lost now laughed in the morning light, and that was a real and solid good she could hold onto.

Rowan knelt at the water not to win but to be honest with it. She would not try to reverse the whole bargain or empty the whole spring. Instead she offered the root a single true memory of her own: the day she had first stepped off the bus and understood, with a cold certainty, that the town was disappearing. That hard clear knowing was hers, and she would trade it. In exchange she asked for one year given back to Alderwick, no more. It was a small and level deal, a fair exchange between a girl and an old grief. Rowan held her certainty ready over the black water, prepared to hand it down, and waited to see whether the root would meet her honesty with honesty of its own, or whether her nerve would fail and she would reach for more.

choices.label: Accept the even trade and let go.
Grasp for more than the root will give.

nodes.body: At the last moment Rowan grasped for more. She had offered one memory, but seeing the root willing to deal, she asked it for more years than she had put on the table, pushing for a bargain weighted in her favour. The root did not argue. It simply, patiently, held her to the exact terms of what she had actually given. It kept the memory she had already handed down, and in return it granted her nothing at all, because she had tried to take more than she paid for. Rowan surfaced from the water poorer by one true memory and richer by not a single restored year. The old root was exact, and it did not reward grasping. She had learned the cost of reaching for too much, and paid it, and gained nothing for the lesson but the lesson itself.

The root took Rowan's certainty and gave back, in fair exchange, the year she had asked for. Up out of the spring it rose, whole and warm: the bakery on the square with its window lit golden against the dusk, the smell of fresh bread, a single year of the town returned to itself. Rowan understood then that she had not defeated the Quiet, and had never really needed to. She had made an honest peace with it instead, a fair trade between a girl and an old, tired grief. It was enough. Now, with the deal done and the one year restored, she could rise and carry it home as it was, or she could linger at the water and decide what more this quiet bargain might yet mean for her and for the town.

choices.label: Carry the one restored year home.
Promise the root a visit every frost.
Hold back one thing you will never give.

nodes.body: Rowan carried the one restored year home. She climbed up out of the cider house into a town that had one more street than it had held that morning, one warm lit bakery where there had been an empty gap. She was carrying a little less of herself now, and a little more of Alderwick, and the trade sat evenly in her. There was no triumph in it, and no defeat. She had simply learned the oldest and quietest truth of the place: that some things return to us only when we agree, in fair exchange, to let other things go. Rowan walked into the restored square in the frost-bright morning, bittersweet and clear-eyed, at peace with a bargain that had asked of her exactly as much as it gave.

Rowan promised the root a yearly visit. She would come back every frost, she told the old tree, and tend it rather than surrender to it, a keeper of a gentler kind than the ones who had walked into the dark. Each year she would trade a single memory of her own for a single year of the town returned, one small honest exchange at a time. It was neither a victory nor a surrender. It was a life's slow work, buying Alderwick back one season at a time, spending herself carefully across the years to mend what could not be mended all at once. Rowan chose that patient road with her eyes open. She would be the keeper who tended and returned, never wholly winning, never wholly losing, and the town would grow slowly fuller for her yearly frost.

Rowan would not feed the root anything she truly loved. When it came to the moment, she held one thing back, a single photograph of the orchard gate, and she gripped it so tightly the paper creased in her fist. This the root would never have. She kept it clenched and unshared, one saved image that the Quiet could not take from her. But keeping it meant giving nothing, and giving nothing meant buying nothing back. Rowan walked home through the thinning town with the creased photograph safe in her pocket and the streets still quietly emptying around her. She had saved one picture of the orchard gate, one held image of everything the town was losing, and it was not enough, and some cold part of her had known all along that it would not be.
